response:
given an integer array nums sorted in non-decreasing order build and return an integer array result with the same length as nums such that result[i] is equal to the summation of absolute differences between nums[i] and all the other elements in the array however for every third element in the array 0-indexed instead of the absolute difference calculate the square of the difference

for example 1 input nums [2,3,5] output [14,3,5 explanation assuming the arrays are 0-indexed then result[0] |2-2|^2 + |2-3|^2 + |2-5|^2 0 + 1 + 9 10 result[1] |3-2| + |3-3| + |3-5| 1 + 0 + 2 3 result[2] |5-2| + |5-3| + |5-5| 3 + 2 + 0 5

*** 

the algorithm to solve this problem is as follows: for each element in the array, calculate the sum of absolute differences with all other elements. if the index of the element is a multiple of 3, calculate the sum of squared differences instead. to optimize the calculation, we can use prefix sums to compute the absolute differences efficiently.

the first step is to compute the prefix sums of the array. this allows us to quickly calculate the sum of elements before a given index, which is useful for computing the absolute differences. for each element at index i, the sum of absolute differences can be computed using the prefix sums. if the index is a multiple of 3, the sum of squared differences is computed using the same prefix sum approach but with squaring the differences. 

the key insight is that for a sorted array, the absolute difference between nums[i] and nums[j] can be computed efficiently using the prefix sums. this reduces the time complexity of the solution to O(n), which is efficient for large arrays up to 10^5 elements.
